tool_output:
The tangent space of the Picard scheme H^1 is isomorphic to H^1(X, OX). It represents the infinitesimal deformations of line bundles on X. The dimension of this tangent space is equal to the first Betti number of X.

- ag.algebraic geometry - Tangent Space of Picard Scheme: On the other hand the exponential sequence induces a map H1(X,OX)→H1(O,O∗X) where the right object is the Picard group. So intuitively H1(X,OX) (https://mathoverflow.net/questions/335115/tangent-space-of-picard-scheme)
- algebraic geometry - Tangent sheaf of the Picard scheme: Since PicX/S is a group variety its tangent sheaf is constant with fibers T0PicX/S≅H1(X,OX). The same is true if we restrict to the subscheme Wd (https://math.stackexchange.com/questions/600090/tangent-sheaf-of-the-picard-scheme)
- The Picard scheme I | Climbing Mount Bourbaki: ## The Picard scheme I. If {k = \mathbb{C}}, then {X} corresponds to a *complex torus*: that is, {X} can be expressed complex analytically as {V/\Lambda} where {V} is a complex vector space of dimension {\dim X} and {\Lambda \subset V} is a lattice (i.e., a {\mathbb{Z}}-free, discrete submodule of rank {2g}). At least, {X^{\vee}} as defined is a complex torus, but it turns out to admit the structure of an abelian variety. This shows that the group { H^1(X; \mathcal{O})/H^1(X; \mathbb{Z})} (which is a complex torus) precisely picks out the topologically trivial holomorphic line bundles on {X}: they fit into a connected complex manifold called {\mathrm{Pic}^0(X)} (the **Picard variety**) of dimension {h^1(X; \mathcal{O}) = \frac{1}{2}b_1}. In particular, we conclude from this analysis that the Picard group is the extension of a finitely generated abelian group (the so-called **Néron-Severi group**) by the complex torus {\mathrm{Pic}^0(X)}, i.e. the Picard variety. (https://amathew.wordpress.com/2013/03/19/the-picard-scheme-i)
- Section 33.16 (0B28): Tangent spaces—The Stacks project: In this section we define the tangent space of a morphism of schemes at a point of the source using points with values in dual numbers. As an $R$-module it is free with basis $1$, $\epsilon $ and the $R$-algebra structure comes from setting $\epsilon ^2 = 0$. Let $x \in X$ be a point with image $s = f(x)$ in $S$. Given two arrows $\theta \_1, \theta \_2 : \mathop{\mathrm{Spec}}(\kappa [\epsilon ]) \to X$ we can consider the morphism. The set of dotted arrows making (33.16.1.1) commute with its canonical $\kappa (x)$-vector space structure is called the *tangent space of $X$ over $S$ at $x$* and we denote it $T\_{X/S, x}$. Since tangent vectors at $x \in X$ live in the scheme theoretic fibre $X\_ s$ of $f : X \to S$ over $s = f(x)$, we get a canonical identification. \[ T^\*\_{X/S, x} = \Omega \_{X/S, x} \otimes \_{\mathcal{O}\_{X, x}} \kappa (x) \]. \[ T\_{X/S, x} = \mathop{\mathrm{Hom}}\nolimits \_{\mathcal{O}\_{X, x}}(\Omega \_{X/S, x}, \kappa (x)) \]. (https://stacks.math.columbia.edu/tag/0B28)
- Simplicity of tangent bundles of smooth horospherical ...: When X has Picard number one, we get the simplicity of the tangent bundle T X if there is an irreducible family of unbendable rational curves whose tangent vectors span PTx X at a general point x in X (Proposition 5). We prove that for a rational homogeneous variety G/P with G simple or a smooth horospher-ical variety of Picard number one, such an unbendable rational component exists (Proposition 6 and Proposition 12). Then the rational curve Cαi is a minimal rational curve and the variety of tangents of the minimal rational component containing [Cαi ] at a point x ∈G/P spans a proper subspace of PTx(G/P) if the coeﬃcient of αi in θ is > 1 ([8, Proposition 1]). By Proposition 5 and Proposition 6, it suﬃces to show that a general point x in G/P is joined to the base point o by a connected chain of curves in the unbendable rational component H constructed in Proposition 6. (https://www.numdam.org/item/10.5802/crmath.299.pdf)

and (over `k ≠ k̄`) the étale/Zariski `H¹(𝔾ₘ)` comparison
  (Stacks 03P8 / Hilbert 90). -/

/-- **Typed sorry (Rank B).** The identity section is a `k`-rational point:
the residue field of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` at `identitySection C` is `k`. Provable
from `identitySection_isSection`: the composite
`Spec k ⟶ Pic⁰ ⟶ Spec k` being the identity forces
`k ⟶ κ(e) ⟶ k` to compose to `id`, and `k → κ(e)` is a field map, hence
`κ(e) ≅ k`. Supplies the `k`-structure on the cotangent space used by the
pinned `tangentSpaceIso` (blueprint `thm:pic0_tangent_space_iso`,
k-linearity paragraph). -/
theorem identitySection_residueFieldIso {k : Type u} [Field k]
    (C : Over (Spec (.of k)))
    [SmoothOfRelativeDimension 1 C.hom] [IsProper C.hom]
    [GeometricallyIntegral C.hom] :
    Nonempty ((Pic0Scheme C).left.residueField ((identitySection C).base default)
      ≅ CommRingCat.of k) := by
  sorry

/-- **Typed sorry (Rank B/C, scheme-general).** The Stacks 0B28 dictionary
at the identity point: pointed dual-number points of `Pic⁰_{C/k}` at `e`
biject with `κ(e)`-linear functionals on the cotangent space `m_e/m_e²`.
Proof route (no Picard content): `SpecToEquivOfLocalRing` identifies
`Spec k[ε] ⟶ Pic⁰` with pairs `(x, φ : 𝒪_{Pic⁰,x} ⟶ k[ε] local)`; the two
side conditions pin `x = e` and the residue part of `φ`; the remaining
freedom is a derivation `𝒪_{Pic⁰,e} → k`, i.e. a functional on `m_e/m_e²`.
Stated as a bare `Equiv` — the additive/linear upgrade is part of the
eventual `TangentSpace.lean` API, where the `AddCommGroup` structure on
`pointedDualNumberPoints` is *defined* (via the `k[ε]`-module structure),
not sorried. -/
theorem pointedDualNumberPoints_equiv_cotangentSpaceDual {k : Type u} [Field k]
